Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd is a company incorporated in Singapore on 5 August 2020 with a paid-up share capital of $100,000.

As at incorporation, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd had three shareholders: Liew Nyok Wah holding 33% of the shares, Teo Ying Ping holding 33% of the shares, and Ho Siow Poh holding 34% of the shares.
From incorporation, Ho Siow Poh was the Managing Director of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd and its sole recorded director.
In 2021, Marcus Teo Xian Jun was appointed Chief Financial Officer of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd.

From the inception of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, Teo Ying Ping and Liew Nyok Wah provided funds to the company, initially from their personal funds and later also using funds obtained from third-party loans either to themselves or directly to the company.
The Accounting and Corporate Regulatory Authority filings and financial statements of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd recorded its share capital as $100,000 during the relevant period.

AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd recorded funds provided by its shareholders in its internal accounting records and financial statements under headings including 'Loan from Director' and in internal 'loan sheets' circulated within its accounting department.
AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd paid interest on certain funds provided by its shareholders at rates that in some instances exceeded 8% per annum.

AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd made repayments of certain sums previously provided by or through shareholder Teo Ying Ping, including payments of $160,000 on 14 January 2021 and $15,000 on 20 December 2022.

AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd made repayments of certain third-party loans associated with funding introduced by its shareholders, including loans from Mengkim Holdings Pte Ltd, Junk Yard Dog Ventures Pte Ltd, Tan Bee Hong, Ban Leong, and others.

On 8 January 2021, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd entered into a loan agreement with Mengkim Holdings Pte Ltd for $500,000 at an interest rate of 10% per annum, signed on behalf of the company by Managing Director Ho Siow Poh.

On 7 April 2021, a sum of $100,000 from shareholder Teo Ying Ping was provided to AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, with a contemporaneous WhatsApp message describing it as a short-term arrangement for two months with interest at 1.5% per month.
AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd obtained a $300,000 loan from Junk Yard Dog Ventures Pte Ltd, which was reflected in the company’s accounting records as a loan from Junk Yard Dog.

On 8 October 2021, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd received approximately $260,000 arising from a hire-purchase loan taken by shareholder Teo Ying Ping over his car, under which a total of $311,740 was to be repaid over 60 monthly instalments of about $5,196.

Following the 8 October 2021 hire-purchase transaction, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd began paying the monthly instalments of about $5,196 on the hire-purchase loan for Teo Ying Ping’s car until the company ran out of funds.

By 8 October 2021, net contributions associated with shareholder funding (excluding initial share capital) recorded in the company’s records were approximately $2,124,407 for Teo Ying Ping and $1,800,000 for Liew Nyok Wah.
AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd periodically repaid portions of the $300,000 loan from Junk Yard Dog Ventures Pte Ltd, including repayments totalling $200,000 between 1 June 2022 and 12 July 2022.

Within the 'AP Accounting Department' WhatsApp chatgroup, which included at least Ms Liew and Marcus Teo, internal 'loan sheets' were circulated showing dates of loans, expiry dates, and monthly loan interest for funds provided to AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd.

On 9 January 2023, Managing Director Ho Siow Poh signed a Creditors Confirmation Request confirming that AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd owed $1.03 million to shareholder Liew Nyok Wah as at 31 July 2022.
AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd prepared updated accounts for adoption at an Annual General Meeting in 2024, reflecting amounts owed to shareholders Liew Nyok Wah and Teo Ying Ping as loans.
In or around May 2023, disagreements arose among the shareholders of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd concerning the company’s funding arrangements.
On 25 July 2023, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd commenced legal proceedings against shareholder Liew Nyok Wah in Originating Claim No 475 of 2023.
On 27 July 2023, the employment of Ms Liew Shulin with AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd was terminated.
After the departure of Ms Liew from AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd in August 2023, Managing Director Ho Siow Poh took over responsibility for the company’s financial documents and accounting records.
As at 24 August 2023, the registered office of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd was changed from premises associated with Jackspeed Automobile (S) Pte Ltd to another address.
AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd paid a monthly retainer fee to Jackspeed Automobile (S) Pte Ltd for office space and related facilities from at least March 2021 to May 2023.

After moving its workshop operations to Loyang in early 2021, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd continued to use office and storage space at the Tampines premises associated with Jackspeed Automobile (S) Pte Ltd, including an office unit, a room, and storage for records, furniture, and a computer.
In 2021 and 2022, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd sold a substantial number of cars from its fleet as part of efforts to improve its financial position and reduce liabilities.

From late 2021 into 2022, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd used proceeds from car sales to repay various loans, including loans from Lee Sze Peng, WQ Leasing Pte Ltd, and Junk Yard Dog Ventures Pte Ltd.

On 2 May 2023, a meeting took place between shareholders of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, including Liew Nyok Wah and Teo Ying Ping, during which funding of the company and potential buy-out figures between $500,000 and $700,000 were discussed in Mandarin.

During 2021 and 2022, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd made repayments on loans associated with funding introduced by or through shareholder Liew Nyok Wah, including loans from individuals named Tan Bee Hong and Ban Leong.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether funds introduced into AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd by or through its shareholders, including Liew Nyok Wah, prior to the dispute should be characterised as shareholder loans or as capital injections, and whether and to what extent such funds were repayable by the company.
Party_Details.Rule: Company funding provided by shareholders may be treated either as equity capital or as debt; the legal characterisation generally depends on the parties’ agreements, the company’s constitutional and statutory framework, and objective indicators such as documentation, accounting treatment, repayment terms, and interest arrangements. Shareholder loans are ordinarily repayable according to their terms, whereas paid-up share capital is not repayable except in accordance with applicable company law procedures.
Party_Details.Application: AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd’s internal and regulatory records maintained its paid-up share capital at $100,000 while recording subsequent shareholder-related funding under loan classifications, with interest being paid on many such sums and with multiple repayments being made to or on behalf of shareholders and third-party lenders. The company entered into formal loan arrangements with external entities such as Mengkim Holdings Pte Ltd and Junk Yard Dog Ventures Pte Ltd, and it treated funds arising from a hire-purchase loan over a shareholder’s car as giving rise to instalment obligations that the company serviced. These objective features align with the treatment of the post-incorporation funding as debt rather than as additional share capital, and they formed the basis for the later dispute over whether the company was obliged to repay amounts associated with funding introduced by or through shareholder Liew Nyok Wah.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge analysed the pleaded case that there was an agreement among the three shareholders for dollar-to-dollar capital injections and found that the evidence did not establish such an agreement. The court noted the absence of written documentation, inconsistencies in the evidence of Managing Director Ho, and the ambiguity in references to 'funds or capital injections'. The judge placed weight on the company’s financial statements and ACRA filings, which consistently showed share capital of $100,000, and on internal accounting records and 'loan sheets' that described shareholder-related funding as loans, including under headings such as 'Loan from Director'. The judge found that interest was paid only on contributions beyond the initial $100,000 share capital and that interest rates sometimes exceeded 8% per annum, which was consistent with loan treatment. The court examined specific transactions, including the $100,000 provided by Teo on 7 April 2021, the $300,000 Junk Yard Dog loan, and the $260,000 hire-purchase-related funding, and concluded that these were loans rather than capital injections. The judge found that Ho’s 'Table' of contributions was inaccurate and presented in a misleading way, and that there was no contemporaneous enforcement of any matching-funds obligation. The court rejected the company’s attempt in closing submissions to recast its case as a 'Matching Dollar Agreement' to fund the company regardless of source, holding that this was unpleaded and unsupported by conduct, which showed ad hoc voluntary contributions and withdrawals. The judge held that, at most, there was an expectation that Teo and Liew would help fund the company, which did not amount to a legal obligation. The court also rejected the implication of a term restricting withdrawals until profitability, distinguishing Wee Kah Lee v Silverdale Investment Pte Ltd on the basis that that case involved a specific development project and a pleaded implied term, whereas here the loans were for general operations and there was no clear temporal trigger such as project completion. On this basis, the judge concluded that the funds provided by or through Liew were loans and that there was no contractual or equitable restriction on their repayment. The court therefore held that Liew was entitled to repayment of his loans and that the company’s claim based on alleged breach of a funding agreement and alleged breach of fiduciary duties could not succeed. The judge further held that the company had not shown that Liew procured repayments in a manner contrary to any duty, noting that Ho, as Managing Director, authorised the repayments and that alleged 'pressure' by Liew was neither pleaded as duress nor supported by evidence. The court rejected the contention that Ho acted under Liew’s directions as a shadow director, as this was not properly pleaded and lacked evidential support. On damages, the judge found the various heads of loss to be misconceived: the interest-rate differential claim was abandoned and in any event inconsistent with the evidence that shareholders, including Teo, received higher rates; the 'fire sale' claim was undermined by the timing of car sales, which began before the Mengkim loan was due, and by evidence that the sale formed part of a restructuring plan proposed by Marcus Teo, with proceeds used to repay multiple loans, not just those associated with Liew; the 'loss of use' calculation based on differences in net profit after tax was described as legally and evidentially unsound, ignoring the timing and quantum of actual repayments and the fact that the company had agreed to repay the loans; the retainer-fee claim against Liew relating to payments to Jackspeed was abandoned and, in any event, inconsistent with the shareholders’ agreement to continue paying a reduced fee for ongoing use of the Tampines premises; and the $300,000 loan to Chris Eng claim was expressly abandoned at trial. Given these findings, the judge considered it unnecessary to decide whether Liew was a de facto or shadow director. On the counterclaim, the court held that, since Liew’s contributions were loans and there was no effective defence based on any matching-funds agreement, Liew was entitled to judgment for the undisputed sum of $719,000. The court dismissed the company’s claim, allowed Liew’s counterclaim, awarded him $150,000 in costs inclusive of disbursements, and indicated that it would hear further submissions on whether shareholder Teo and Managing Director Ho should bear personal liability for certain costs.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Liew Nyok Wah

Facts.Text: Liew Nyok Wah was a shareholder of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd holding 33% of its issued shares from incorporation on 5 August 2020.
Before and during the incorporation of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, Liew Nyok Wah, Ho Siow Poh, and Teo Ying Ping were long-term friends who decided to start a business venture together.
During the relevant period, Liew Nyok Wah was not recorded as a director of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd in official filings, although he was actively involved in funding discussions and decisions.
Liew Nyok Wah is the father of Liew Shulin, who worked in AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd’s finance and business operations shortly after incorporation.

From the inception of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, Liew Nyok Wah provided funds to the company, initially from his personal funds and later also by arranging loans from third parties either to himself or directly to the company.

By 8 October 2021, net funding associated with or arranged by Liew Nyok Wah for AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, excluding his initial $50,000 share capital, was approximately $1.8 million according to the company’s records.

AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd’s internal accounting records and a Creditors Confirmation Request signed on 9 January 2023 recorded that the company owed approximately $1.03 million to Liew Nyok Wah as at 31 July 2022.

Liew Nyok Wah arranged for AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd to obtain a $500,000 loan from Mengkim Holdings Pte Ltd on 8 January 2021, which was documented in a loan agreement signed by Managing Director Ho Siow Poh.

Liew Nyok Wah arranged for funding to AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd through individuals including Tan Bee Hong and Ban Leong, with the funds being recorded in the company’s accounts under 'Loan from Director' categories associated with him.

During 2021 and 2022, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd made repayments of principal and interest on loans associated with funding introduced by or through Liew Nyok Wah, including repayments on the Mengkim loan and loans from individuals such as Tan Bee Hong and Ban Leong.

On 2 May 2023, during a recorded meeting with other shareholders, Liew Nyok Wah discussed the extent of his funding contributions to AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, possible matching of contributions with Teo Ying Ping, and potential buy-out figures between $500,000 and $700,000.

Throughout the life of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd up to the dispute in May 2023, Liew Nyok Wah participated in decisions regarding the company’s funding structure, including the use of third-party loans and the repayment of such loans.
By around May 2023, the relationship between Liew Nyok Wah and the other shareholders of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd had deteriorated due to disagreements over funding arrangements.

On 25 July 2023, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd commenced legal proceedings against Liew Nyok Wah, and he subsequently filed a counterclaim seeking repayment of sums recorded as loans from him to the company.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the sums recorded in AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd’s accounts as owing to or associated with funding introduced by or through shareholder Liew Nyok Wah constituted repayable loans, and whether the company was obliged to repay those sums to him.
Party_Details.Rule: Where a shareholder advances funds to a company or arranges for third-party loans in connection with the company’s operations, the legal characterisation of those advances as debt or equity depends on the parties’ agreements and objective indicators such as documentation, accounting treatment, interest terms, and patterns of repayment. If such advances are properly characterised as loans, the company is generally obliged to repay them in accordance with their terms, any valid contractual or statutory restrictions.
Party_Details.Application: AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd’s internal accounting records, financial statements, and a signed Creditors Confirmation Request identified substantial sums associated with funding introduced by or through shareholder Liew Nyok Wah as loans owed by the company. The company entered into a formal loan agreement with Mengkim Holdings Pte Ltd for $500,000 arranged by Liew, and it recorded other funding associated with him under 'Loan from Director' categories. The company paid interest on these sums and made repayments over time, consistent with loan treatment. There was no contemporaneous documentation establishing that these funds were additional share capital or that they were non-repayable, and the company’s share capital remained at $100,000 in its regulatory filings. In the absence of a binding funding agreement restricting repayment, these objective features supported treating the sums as loans repayable to or on behalf of Liew, forming the basis of his counterclaim for repayment.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge considered the company’s claim that Liew was bound by an agreement to provide matching capital injections and that he had acted contrary to that agreement by using third-party loans and causing repayments that allegedly harmed the company. The court rejected the existence of any binding capital-injection or matching-funds agreement for the reasons set out in the analysis of the company’s claim, including the lack of written evidence, inconsistent testimony from Ho, and the company’s own accounting treatment of the funds as loans. The judge found that the company’s attempt to reframe its case as a 'Matching Dollar Agreement' in closing submissions was unpleaded and inconsistent with the parties’ conduct, which showed voluntary, ad hoc contributions and withdrawals without enforcement of any matching obligation. The court held that, at most, there was an informal understanding that Liew and Teo would help fund the company, which did not amount to a legal obligation restricting repayment. On the characterisation of Liew’s contributions, the judge relied on the company’s financial statements, ACRA filings, internal 'loan sheets', and the 9 January 2023 Creditors Confirmation Request signed by Ho, all of which treated the sums as loans. The court noted that interest was paid on these sums and that the company made repayments over time, including on the Mengkim loan and loans from individuals such as Tan Bee Hong and Ban Leong. The judge concluded that these objective indicators supported treating Liew’s contributions as loans rather than capital. The company’s arguments that Liew had caused or procured repayments in breach of fiduciary duties were rejected, as Ho, the Managing Director, had authorised the repayments and there was no pleaded or proven basis such as duress or undue influence. The court found no evidential foundation for the assertion that Ho acted under Liew’s directions as a shadow director. Since the company’s defences based on an alleged funding agreement and alleged breach of duties failed, the judge held that there was no substantive defence to Liew’s counterclaim for repayment of his loans. The company did not dispute the quantum of $719,000 claimed by Liew as outstanding. Accordingly, the court allowed Liew’s counterclaim and awarded him judgment for $719,000, together with costs of $150,000 inclusive of disbursements, while dismissing the company’s claim.
